original_abstract: A central challenge in electrochemical carbon dioxide reduction is the development of catalytic materials that combine high product selectivity and long-term stability with a composition of earth-abundant elements. Here, we present thin films of nanosized metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) as atomically defined, nanostructured catalysts capable of the efficient and selective reduction of $CO_2$ to CO in aqueous electrolytes. Investigating a cobalt-porphyrin MOF, $Al_2(OH)_2TCPP-Co$, we demonstrate a selectivity for CO production exceeding 76% and sustained stability over 7 hours, achieving a per-site turnover number (TON) of 1400. In situ spectroelectrochemical analysis offered a real-time window into the cobalt oxidation states, revealing that most catalytic centers within the MOF are redox-accessible and that the reduction of Co(II) to Co(I) drives the catalytic cycle.
humanized_abstract: Preparation of High-Selectivity, Earth-Abundant CO2 Reduction Catalysts with Stability over Weeks Electrochemical reduction of carbon dioxide to usable chemicals and fuels is becoming a critical field of research due to its immense potential to address increasing energy demand while reducing the environmental impacts of combustion-related processes. However, the main challenge in this area is to find a catalyst that will have both a high selectivity to the product of interest and stability under operational conditions. All known catalytic materials currently have some aspect of their structure and composition that limits their practical use in this area of research, most often due to the use of expensive metals. We show that films of nanoscale metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) represent a new class of materials for this field of research, namely that of atomically defined nanostructures for the electrochemical reduction of carbon dioxide. The cobalt-porphyrin MOF material, Al2(OH)2TCPP-Co, exhibited CO selectivity over 76% and stability of at least 7 h (achieved a per site turnover number (TON) of 1400). In situ spectroelectrochemical experiments showed that almost all of the cobalt ions in the film are redox active, with Co2+ being reduced to Co1+ in the catalytic reduction of CO2 to CO, as hypothesized.
